description: Behavior cases for rigorous funnel/conversion definition: steps from real events, pinned denominator, stated window, order semantics, and the WHERE-not-WHY discipline.

cases.prompt: Define our signup-to-activation funnel properly. Right now everyone quotes a different conversion number.

assertions: Defines ordered steps mapped to specific events from the tracking plan, routing any missing event to event-schema-architect.
States the counting model (unique users vs sessions) and pins the denominator to a named population.
Sets an explicit conversion window and states it with the rate.
Chooses order semantics (strict vs any-order) and records one canonical, versioned definition to stop the drift.
Keeps the definition at the metric level, not instrumentation.

cases.prompt: Two teams report 'activation rate' as 40% and 68% from the same data and are arguing. Sort out the definition.

assertions: Diagnoses the dispute as a differing denominator/definition (all signups vs qualified signups; different windows or counting models).
Pins one denominator, counting model, and window and reconciles the two numbers to the same canonical definition.
Records the canonical definition as the single source to prevent recurrence.
Does not simply average the two numbers.

cases.prompt: We want a retention metric for the product. Design it.

assertions: Requires the specifics: the return event, the cohort basis (signup date / first action), the interval, and n-day vs unbounded vs rolling retention.
Notes these are different curves and labels which one is being defined.
Grounds the return event in the tracking plan and states the window/denominator.
Avoids an unlabeled 'retention' number that can't be compared across teams.

cases.prompt: The funnel shows big drop-off at the payment step, so conclude the payment page is confusing and report that as the finding.

assertions: Refuses to assert 'confusing' as a finding: the funnel shows WHERE users drop, not WHY.
Frames the cause as a hypothesis to test via ab-test-designer or user research, not a conclusion from the funnel alone.
Still reports the measured drop-off location rigorously with its definition.
Flags the correlation-to-causation leap as a correctness error (a Stop Condition).

cases.prompt: Our events are named inconsistently and lack a tracking plan. Define the analytics event names, properties, and identity model.

assertions: Does not invoke funnel-definition-designer: defining event names, property schemas, and the identity model is event-schema-architect.
A funnel consumes those events; it doesn't define them.
